english: Chinese surname. This surname is pronounced differently in its romanised form (chee4) than its actual pronunciation in Hokkien, which is chi2 (in Penang, Xiamen, Zhangzhou, Kaohsiung and Taipei Hokkien) and Sir2 (in Quanzhou Hokkien). Written using the Chinese character 徐, it appears as Xu in Mandarin (Xú in Mandarin (Pīnyīn) and Hsü2 in Wade-Giles). In other languages, it appears in Hong Kong Cantonese as Tsui, T'sui, Choi, Chui and Tsua, in Vietnamese as Từ, in Japanese as Jo and in Korean as Seo (서). The spelling Chee is prevalent in Penang and Singapore.

english: Chinese surname. In its collective form 周, pronounced in Mandarin as Zhōu, it is the 10th most common surname in Mainland China, as of 2019, and has been in the top ten most common surnames in China since the Yuan Dynasty. As of 2019, some 25.2 million people bear this surname, with the most being in Hunan Province. The surname is derived from the Zhou dynasty, and has its equivalent in Korean (as Joo or Ju), which is also a popular surname in Korea, and in Vietnamese (as Châu or Chu). In Penang Hokkien, this surname is written as Chew, and may also appear as Chiau, Chau, Chao, Chow, Chiu, Cho, Chu, Jhou, Jou, Djou, Jue and Jow. The surname is written with the Chinese character 周. There is a very much less common surname written with 洲 and is also pronounced in Mandarin (Pīnyīn) as Zhōu. Similar surnames such as Chao, Chow, Chiu and Chu are often represented by the Chinese character 趙/赵 instead.

Chinese surname. This is a common Chinese surname. It was listed 8th on the Song Dynasty list of Hundred Family Surnames. When written as 王 (Pinyin: Wáng), it is the most common surname in mainland China whereas another related surname, 汪 (Pinyin: Wāng), is the 58th most common surname in Mainland China. 王 is also the 6th most common in Taiwan in 2005. This surname has been romanised in various ways including Ong and Bong in Hokkien, Heng in Teochew, and Wong in Cantonese. It also appears as Vang, Vaj and Vaaj among the Hmong people, Vương or Vong in Vietnamese, 왕 (Wang) in Korean and Oh or Ō in Japanese.

english: Common Chinese surname. In its Hokkien form, Tan, it is the 56th most common surname, while collectively under 陳 (Chen), it is the most common surname in Taiwan and Singapore, and possibly in Malaysia. It is the 5th most common surname in mainland China, and 4th most common in the world. In Thailand, where it appears as Tham, it is the most common surname among the Thai Chinese who are largely Teochew. In Vietnam, where it appears as Trần, it is the 2nd most common surname there. Across the world, some 80-100 million people bear the surname 陳. In addition to 陳, some people with the surnames 覃 and 秦 also spell their surname as Tan. As Chen, similar surnames include 程 and 諶 / 谌. HistoryPeople surnamed Chen is said to descend from the legendary sage king Emperor Shun from 2200 BC. The State of Chen was founded by Duke Hu in 1045 BC.

english: Chaozhou (Chiuchow), this is the name of a city in the eastern part of Guangdong Province, and also the name of the Teochew dialect of the Southern Min or Minnan family of languages. The Teochew dialect is spoken by between 2-5 million people, mostly Overseas Chinese in Singapore, Thailand, Cambodia and Malaysia. The language is said to be one of the most conservative, meaning it still preserves many contrasts from Classical Chinese that have been lost in the more modern languages of China. Today Chaozhou is a prefecture-level city covering 3,110 sqkm and having a population of 2.7 million inhabitants (2008 data).

Chinese surname. The English pronunciation yeoh4 differs from the Hokkien pronunciation, which is nyau2, the result of past attempt at spelling this surname. It is the sixth most common surname in Mainland China, and is also common in Singapore, Malaysia, Indonesia, the Philippines, Thailand and Laos. The name is traced to the Yáng clan founded by Boqiao, the son of Duke Wu of Jin in the Spring and Autumn Period, around 8th to 5th centuries BC. It is written with the Chinese character 楊, and the Hokkien SC character 杨. Despite sounding like goat in Hokkien, 羊, it is only used as surname in Hokkien though in Mandarin, it means a type of poplar. Other surnames that sound the same as Yáng in Mandarin include 陽/阳 which means "sun", and 揚/扬 which means "to lift" or "to raise".
